paragraph: Researchers at the Halvorsen Institute for Translational Oncology in Vestmark City announced results from the CARDINAL-3 trial, a phase III randomized study evaluating the checkpoint inhibitor zorvelimab in combination with standard platinum-based chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in patients with previously untreated advanced non-small cell lung cancer. The trial enrolled 612 patients across 38 clinical sites between March 2021 and November 2023. The combination arm demonstrated a median overall survival of 22.4 months compared with 14.7 months in the control arm, corresponding to a hazard ratio of 0.61 and a two-sided p-value of 0.0003. The objective response rate reached 58.9 percent in the experimental group versus 31.2 percent with chemotherapy alone. Grade 3 or higher treatment-related adverse events occurred in 47.3 percent of patients receiving zorvelimab plus chemotherapy compared with 39.1 percent in the control arm, with immune-mediated pneumonitis representing the most frequently observed serious toxicity at 6.8 percent. The findings were presented at the Westbourne International Oncology Symposium and simultaneously published in the journal Precision Cancer Reports.

A multicenter phase II study conducted by the Drexford Cancer Cooperative Group evaluated the anti-PD-L2 monoclonal antibody celestimab in patients with relapsed or refractory microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer who had received at least two prior lines of systemic therapy. The CERULEAN-II trial enrolled 189 participants at 24 centers in the Nordian Federation between January 2022 and August 2024. Celestimab was administered intravenously at 480 mg every four weeks. Among the 177 evaluable patients, the overall response rate was 19.2 percent, with a median duration of response of 8.3 months. Median progression-free survival was 4.1 months, and median overall survival reached 11.6 months. Circulating tumor DNA clearance at week eight was identified as a significant predictor of response, with clearance occurring in 71 percent of responders versus 18 percent of non-responders. The authors, led by principal investigator Dr. Margolaine Tervel of the Drexford Comprehensive Cancer Center, concluded that celestimab offers clinically meaningful activity in this difficult-to-treat population and that ctDNA dynamics may serve as an early pharmacodynamic biomarker to guide treatment decisions.

The Kaluven Oncology Research Consortium released interim results from the SUMMIT-CAR trial, a first-in-human phase I dose-escalation study of KVC-891, an autologous CD19/CD22 dual-targeted chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, in adult patients with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Thirty-one patients were treated across four dose levels ranging from 50 to 400 million CAR-T cells per kilogram of body weight. Complete remission was achieved in 22 of 31 patients, representing a complete remission rate of 71 percent, with 18 of those patients achieving minimal residual disease negativity by next-generation sequencing. The median time to response was 29 days. At a median follow-up of 11.7 months, the six-month event-free survival rate was 58.4 percent. Cytokine release syndrome of any grade occurred in 87 percent of patients, though grade 3 or higher events were observed in only 16.1 percent. Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome of grade 3 or higher was reported in 9.7 percent of participants. The study was led by hematologist Dr. Oswin Fjelstad at the Kaluven University Medical Center and the results were submitted for peer review to the journal Advances in Cellular Immunotherapy.

Investigators at the Meredith-Bauer Cancer Research Institute in Thorncliffe presented data from a phase Ib expansion cohort evaluating IXM-447, a novel small-molecule inhibitor of the SHP2 phosphatase, in combination with the MEK inhibitor cobrettinib in patients with KRAS-mutant pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The combination was assessed in 56 patients who had progressed on one or two prior lines of therapy. IXM-447 was administered orally at 60 mg twice daily while cobrettinib was dosed at 45 mg once daily in 21-day cycles. The confirmed overall response rate was 22 percent, including one complete response and eleven partial responses. Median progression-free survival was 5.8 months, and median overall survival was 9.4 months, compared to a historical control median overall survival of approximately 6.5 months in this population. The most common treatment-emergent adverse events of any grade included diarrhea at 68 percent, peripheral edema at 54 percent, and dermatologic rash at 46 percent. Translational analyses showed that baseline allelic frequency of KRAS G12D and G12V mutations correlated inversely with depth of response, an observation with potential implications for patient selection strategies.

A landmark biomarker-stratified phase III trial known as HARBOR-ADAPT, sponsored by the Crestwood Pharmaceutical Research Foundation, demonstrated that the addition of onralizumab, a bispecific antibody targeting PD-1 and LAG-3 simultaneously, to standard first-line chemotherapy significantly improved outcomes in patients with PD-L1-high metastatic cervical cancer. The trial enrolled 420 women across 51 sites in nine countries between February 2020 and June 2023. In the PD-L1 combined positive score 10 or greater subgroup, representing 54 percent of enrolled patients, the experimental arm achieved a median overall survival of 28.7 months versus 16.3 months in the control arm, yielding a hazard ratio of 0.54 and a p-value below 0.0001. The objective response rate in this subgroup was 67.4 percent. Notably, the benefit was not observed in patients with a combined positive score below 10, underscoring the predictive value of PD-L1 expression in this indication. The safety profile was consistent with previously reported data for each individual agent, with a 3.1 percent rate of treatment-related discontinuation in the experimental arm.

Scientists at Veltramex Biosciences reported preclinical and early clinical data supporting the development of VTX-2291, a lutetium-177 labeled fibroblast activation protein inhibitor radiopharmaceutical, for the treatment of patients with solid tumors exhibiting high stromal FAP expression. In a phase I dose-finding study enrolling 42 patients with FAP-positive pancreatic, ovarian, and colorectal cancers at the Almeira Oncology Center in Belforte, VTX-2291 was administered at activities ranging from 3.7 to 9.3 gigabecquerels per cycle for four cycles given six weeks apart. Dosimetric modeling indicated favorable absorbed doses to tumor tissue relative to critical organs, with mean tumor absorbed dose of 42 Gy per cycle at the 7.4 GBq level. Partial responses were confirmed in 9 of 38 evaluable patients, with stable disease in an additional 17. The disease control rate was 68.4 percent at the recommended phase II dose of 7.4 GBq. The most common adverse events were mild-to-moderate fatigue in 71 percent of patients and grade 1 to 2 xerostomia in 52 percent. Grade 3 thrombocytopenia was observed in 11.9 percent of patients at the highest dose level, prompting dose de-escalation for three participants.

The NEOVAX-BC Consortium, a collaborative research program funded by the Stellarion Health Foundation, reported results from a personalized neoantigen cancer vaccine trial in patients with high-risk resected triple-negative breast cancer. The pilot study, designated NVC-TNBC-01, enrolled 28 patients at four academic medical centers in the Aurentian Republic. Each participant received a customized multi-epitope mRNA neoantigen vaccine manufactured within 74 days of tumor resection, administered in combination with a fixed regimen of the immune adjuvant tolmarizide. At median follow-up of 18 months, the two-year invasive disease-free survival rate was 78.6 percent, compared with a matched historical control rate of 61 percent derived from institutional registry data. Immune monitoring demonstrated neoantigen-specific T-cell responses in 24 of 28 patients as measured by interferon-gamma enzyme-linked immunospot assay. The breadth of the vaccine-induced T-cell response, defined as the number of recognized epitopes per patient, was significantly correlated with event-free survival, with each additional recognized epitope associated with a 23 percent reduction in hazard. Investigators concluded that the approach is feasible and potentially immunogenic, warranting further evaluation in a randomized phase II setting.

A phase II single-arm study led by Dr. Hannelore Quistgaard at the Flemontix Institute of Cancer Biology evaluated the TIGIT-blocking antibody tiralumab-C in combination with the IDO1 inhibitor epacindole in patients with recurrent platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian cancer. Sixty-four patients were enrolled between April 2022 and January 2024 at the Flemontix Academic Medical Center in Grevenmoor. The regimen consisted of tiralumab-C at 600 mg intravenously every three weeks combined with epacindole at 400 mg orally twice daily on days one through fourteen of each 21-day cycle. The confirmed objective response rate was 26.6 percent, with a complete response rate of 6.3 percent. Median progression-free survival was 5.3 months and median overall survival was 13.7 months. Biomarker analysis demonstrated that patients with high baseline tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte density had an objective response rate of 42.9 percent versus 15.2 percent in patients with low TIL density, supporting a predictive role for the immune microenvironment. Immune-related adverse events of grade 3 or greater occurred in 28.1 percent of patients, with colitis representing the most common serious event at 12.5 percent.

Liquid biopsy technology developed by Genomelux Diagnostics was validated in a prospective multi-institutional study involving 834 patients with newly diagnosed localized non-small cell lung cancer enrolled across the PRISM-Liquid consortium. The study evaluated the performance of the GLX-cfDNA panel, a 72-gene circulating tumor DNA assay, in predicting recurrence risk following surgical resection with curative intent. Among patients with detectable ctDNA at four weeks post-resection, the two-year recurrence rate was 78.3 percent, versus 14.6 percent in ctDNA-undetectable patients, corresponding to a hazard ratio for recurrence of 6.9 and a p-value below 0.0001. The assay demonstrated a sensitivity of 89.4 percent and specificity of 93.1 percent for detecting recurrence at least 5.2 months before radiographic evidence on standard imaging. In a prespecified exploratory analysis, ctDNA-positive patients who received adjuvant targeted therapy based on molecular alterations identified in the panel showed a numerically lower recurrence rate compared to those who received standard chemotherapy, though this comparison was not powered for statistical significance. The findings were reported in the journal Molecular Cancer Surveillance.

Investigators at the Sandoval-Kressler Cancer Genomics Center reported results from the PATCHWORK-1 trial, a phase II basket study of the pan-FGFR inhibitor filtanertinib in patients with FGFR-amplified or FGFR-mutated solid tumors across multiple histologies. The study enrolled 203 patients with confirmed FGFR alterations identified by central next-generation sequencing, spanning nine tumor types including bladder, gastric, biliary tract, endometrial, breast, glioblastoma, head and neck, lung, and esophageal cancers. Filtanertinib was administered at 20 mg orally once daily in 28-day cycles. The overall response rate across all histologies was 24.6 percent, with the highest response rates observed in the FGFR3-mutant bladder cancer cohort at 43.7 percent and the FGFR2-fusion biliary tract cancer cohort at 39.1 percent. Median duration of response across the entire study population was 7.9 months. Hyperphosphatemia, a class effect of FGFR inhibitors, occurred in 81 percent of patients and was managed with dietary phosphate restriction and phosphate binders. One case of grade 4 central serous retinopathy was reported, requiring permanent treatment discontinuation.

The results of CORTEX-IMMUNE-5, a randomized phase III trial assessing the CTLA-4 inhibitor ipilevimab administered at two dose levels in combination with the PD-1 inhibitor vorstimumab in patients with metastatic uveal melanoma, were published by the Ortegon Melanoma Research Collaborative. Three hundred and six patients were randomized two-to-one to receive ipilevimab at 3 mg per kilogram plus vorstimumab versus vorstimumab monotherapy. The combination arm demonstrated a median overall survival of 19.8 months compared with 12.4 months in the monotherapy arm, with a hazard ratio of 0.63 and p-value of 0.0017. Liver metastases were present at baseline in 78 percent of the patient population, and the treatment benefit was consistent in this subgroup. Grade 3 to 4 immune-related adverse events were substantially more frequent in the combination arm at 54.1 percent versus 17.8 percent with monotherapy. Hepatotoxicity was the predominant serious adverse event, occurring in 19.7 percent of patients in the ipilevimab-plus-vorstimumab group and requiring systemic immunosuppression in 14.2 percent. Despite the toxicity profile, the authors indicated that the survival benefit supports the combination as a potential new standard of care.

A first-in-class antibody-drug conjugate designated PDC-8810, developed by Peristerra Oncologics, was evaluated in a phase I/II study at the Hartwell Research and Treatment Institute in Caeldmont. PDC-8810 links a humanized anti-HER3 antibody to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload via a cleavable linker, and was assessed in patients with HER3-expressing advanced solid tumors regardless of HER2 status. The dose-escalation phase enrolled 58 patients across eight dose levels from 1.2 to 8.4 mg per kilogram administered every three weeks. The recommended phase II dose was determined to be 6.4 mg per kilogram based on pharmacokinetic and safety data. In the expansion cohort of 94 patients with HER3-high metastatic breast cancer, the confirmed objective response rate was 38.3 percent, with a median progression-free survival of 8.1 months. Interstitial lung disease, a known class effect of topoisomerase I payloads, was observed in 12.8 percent of patients across all grades, with grade 3 or higher events occurring in 4.3 percent. Drug-related nausea was the most frequently reported adverse event at 64.9 percent.

Researchers from the Vanthorpe Institute of Hematologic Malignancies published findings from the ALCYONE-2 trial, a randomized phase III study comparing the CD38-targeting antibody dalotuzumab plus lenorexide and dexamethasone against lenorexide and dexamethasone alone in transplant-ineligible newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients. The trial enrolled 706 patients at 62 centers in the Crestland Health Alliance network between September 2019 and March 2022. Median progression-free survival in the three-drug combination arm was 34.6 months versus 18.9 months in the doublet arm, yielding a hazard ratio of 0.50 and a highly significant p-value below 0.00001. The complete response or better rate was 43.6 percent with the triplet versus 22.1 percent with the doublet. Minimal residual disease negativity at a sensitivity of 10 to the minus 5 was achieved in 31.7 percent of triplet-treated patients. The rates of grade 3 or higher infections, including pneumonia and sepsis, were 24.3 percent in the experimental arm versus 17.8 percent in the control arm. Infusion-related reactions to dalotuzumab were predominantly grade 1 to 2 and occurred in 38.9 percent of patients during the first infusion.

The Ovaren Cancer Trials Network announced completion of the SOLSTICE phase II study evaluating WNT-pathway inhibitor galtanidib in patients with desmoid tumors harboring CTNNB1 mutations confirmed by tumor sequencing. Fifty-eight patients with progressive or symptomatic disease were enrolled at 19 sites across the Ovaren Federation and treated with galtanidib at 80 mg orally once daily in 28-day cycles. The primary endpoint of objective response rate per RECIST 1.1 criteria was met, with confirmed partial responses in 20 of 58 patients, yielding a response rate of 34.5 percent. An additional 29 patients achieved stable disease, resulting in a disease control rate of 84.5 percent. Median duration of response had not been reached at the data cutoff, with 13 of 20 responders maintaining response at 12 months. The median progression-free survival was 16.2 months. The most notable adverse events included nausea in 55.2 percent of patients, fatigue in 48.3 percent, and reversible alopecia in 34.5 percent. No skeletal-related adverse events attributable to WNT pathway inhibition were observed, a concern arising from preclinical models. These results support a planned phase III confirmatory registration study.

The POLARIS-LUNG consortium, comprising 16 academic cancer centers across the Greater Arctian Region, reported interim findings from an adaptive platform trial assessing four experimental arms against a shared chemotherapy backbone in patients with stage IIIB to IV squamous cell lung carcinoma. The POLARIS-4 arm, evaluating the STING agonist prodrug STX-3104 in combination with the PD-L1 inhibitor avelomab-N, enrolled 148 patients and demonstrated an objective response rate of 44.6 percent, compared with 27.3 percent in the platform control arm at the time of interim analysis. The hazard ratio for progression-free survival was 0.67 in favor of STX-3104 plus avelomab-N, with a median progression-free survival of 7.3 months versus 4.8 months in the control group. The POLARIS-4 arm achieved the prespecified threshold for superior activity and was advanced to a phase III confirmation study. Treatment-related grade 3 or higher adverse events were reported in 39.2 percent of patients in this arm. Inflammatory reactions at the injection site were common, occurring in 57.4 percent of patients receiving intratumoral STX-3104 administration, though systemic toxicity was manageable.

Scientists at the Zaragovian Molecular Therapeutics Institute presented data from a first-in-human phase I study of ZMT-601, a proteolysis-targeting chimera designed to degrade KRAS G12C oncoprotein through recruitment of the VHL E3 ubiquitin ligase. Forty patients with KRAS G12C-mutant advanced solid tumors, predominantly non-small cell lung cancer and colorectal cancer, were enrolled across three dose cohorts ranging from 200 to 800 mg orally twice daily. Pharmacodynamic analysis confirmed greater than 90 percent KRAS G12C protein degradation in tumor biopsies at the 600 mg dose level in six of eight evaluable patients. Confirmed partial responses were observed in 8 of 36 evaluable patients across all dose levels, with an additional 14 patients achieving stable disease. The maximum tolerated dose was not reached, and the recommended phase II dose was defined as 600 mg twice daily based on pharmacokinetic modeling. Dose-limiting toxicities included grade 3 elevated alanine aminotransferase in two patients at the 800 mg level. Unlike covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors, no emergence of on-target resistance mutations in KRAS was detected in serial circulating tumor DNA samples through 16 weeks of treatment.

A phase III trial designated CRITERION-HR, coordinated by the Bellcroft Breast Cancer Collaborative, evaluated the addition of the CDK4/6 inhibitor ribovexib to fulvestrant in patients with hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer who had progressed on or after a prior aromatase inhibitor. Eight hundred and forty-two postmenopausal women were randomized one-to-one across 89 centers in the Bellcroft Health Cooperative. Median progression-free survival was 16.4 months in the ribovexib-plus-fulvestrant arm versus 9.3 months with fulvestrant alone, corresponding to a hazard ratio of 0.56 and a p-value below 0.0001. The objective response rate was 32.8 percent versus 16.9 percent, and the clinical benefit rate was 78.2 percent versus 61.7 percent. Overall survival data remained immature at the time of reporting but showed a favorable trend with a hazard ratio of 0.83. The most common adverse events associated with ribovexib were neutropenia in 78.3 percent of patients, of which grade 3 to 4 events occurred in 59.1 percent, and fatigue in 41.2 percent. Febrile neutropenia occurred in only 1.7 percent of patients owing to protocol-mandated complete blood count monitoring and dose adjustments.

Researchers from the Nordacian Institute of Translational Medicine published findings from the TITANIUM trial, a phase II window-of-opportunity study evaluating the tumor microenvironment-remodeling agent relvitaclast in combination with neoadjuvant chemotherapy in patients with operable stage II to III gastric adenocarcinoma. Eighty-nine patients were enrolled at six centers in the Nordacian capital, Fjordvik, between May 2021 and December 2023. Relvitaclast, an anti-CXCL12 monoclonal antibody, was administered at 750 mg intravenously every two weeks beginning two weeks prior to the initiation of docetaxel, oxaliplatin, and fluorouracil chemotherapy. The pathological complete response rate was 21.3 percent, compared with a historical benchmark of 12 percent for chemotherapy alone in this population. Tumor infiltrating lymphocyte density in post-treatment resection specimens was 3.4-fold higher in patients receiving relvitaclast compared with a matched contemporaneous chemotherapy-only cohort. The R0 resection rate was 91 percent. Grade 3 or higher adverse events potentially attributable to relvitaclast included hypertension in 11.2 percent and proteinuria in 6.7 percent of patients. The study met its primary endpoint and a randomized phase III study is planned.

A comprehensive genomic profiling study conducted across 1,847 patients with high-grade glioblastoma at the Meridian Neuro-Oncology Institute identified PTPRZ1-MET gene fusions as a targetable alteration in 6.8 percent of newly diagnosed cases. A subsequent phase I basket trial, FUSE-GBM-01, enrolled 24 patients with PTPRZ1-MET fusion-positive glioblastoma to receive the selective MET inhibitor carvetinib-H at 200 mg orally twice daily following standard radiotherapy and temozolomide. Median progression-free survival from start of carvetinib-H was 7.1 months, and six patients remained progression-free at twelve months. Among 20 patients with adequate paired cerebrospinal fluid and plasma samples, carvetinib-H achieved a cerebrospinal fluid-to-plasma ratio of 0.41, suggesting adequate blood-brain barrier penetration. Confirmed partial responses were observed in 6 of 22 evaluable patients, a response rate of 27.3 percent. Two complete responses, confirmed by two consecutive MRI scans six weeks apart, were observed in patients with isolated local recurrence. The most common adverse events were peripheral edema in 54 percent and elevated liver enzymes in 41 percent of patients. These findings were published in the journal Neuro-Oncology Precision.

The first results from the EMERGE-OVCA study, a phase II randomized trial conducted by the Portsmith Gynecologic Oncology Group, evaluated the combination of the PARP inhibitor niramanib with the ATR inhibitor belozanertib in patients with platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer, regardless of BRCA mutational status. One hundred and twelve patients were randomized two-to-one to niramanib plus belozanertib versus niramanib monotherapy. In the intention-to-treat population, the objective response rate was 31.6 percent with the combination versus 14.9 percent with monotherapy, and median progression-free survival was 5.7 months versus 3.4 months, with a hazard ratio of 0.61 and p-value of 0.014. Notably, the combination demonstrated activity regardless of homologous recombination deficiency status, with response rates of 33.8 percent in HRD-negative tumors versus 28.9 percent in HRD-positive tumors. The most common toxicities in the combination arm were anemia in 61.4 percent, thrombocytopenia in 57.9 percent, and nausea in 48.7 percent. Grade 3 or higher hematologic adverse events led to dose reductions in 34.2 percent of patients receiving the combination. Translational studies are ongoing to characterize replication stress biomarkers that may predict sensitivity to ATR inhibition.

Investigators at the Caelstrom Center for Immunotherapy and Cell Engineering reported phase I results for CSCE-101, an allogeneic natural killer cell therapy derived from umbilical cord blood and engineered to express a high-affinity CD16 variant alongside a novel chimeric antigen receptor targeting GD2. The therapy was evaluated in patients with relapsed or refractory neuroblastoma, Ewing sarcoma, and osteosarcoma in a dose-escalation study spanning four dose cohorts from 50 to 600 million cells per infusion. Thirty-three patients received at least one dose. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 51.5 percent of patients and was predominantly grade 1 to 2, with only one case of grade 3 CRS managed with tocilizumab. No cases of immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome were observed. Among 29 evaluable patients, the objective response rate was 31 percent, with three complete responses and six partial responses. The median duration of response was 5.4 months. Persistence of CSCE-101 cells in peripheral blood, measured by flow cytometry, was detectable at day 28 in 72.7 percent of patients and at day 56 in 48.5 percent. The manufacturing turnaround time from order placement to product release was 18 calendar days, demonstrating the logistical advantage of off-the-shelf allogeneic cell therapy.

A pivotal phase III study, RAMPART-HC, sponsored by the Vellindra Biopharmaceutical Group, assessed the hepatocellular carcinoma-directed regimen of the anti-VEGFR2 antibody bezindanib in combination with the PD-1 inhibitor stivimab versus sorafenibe, an established tyrosine kinase inhibitor used as active comparator, in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma who had not received prior systemic therapy. Eight hundred and twenty patients were enrolled at 74 centers. In the primary endpoint analysis, the combination arm demonstrated median overall survival of 24.1 months versus 14.6 months with sorafenibe, corresponding to a hazard ratio of 0.59 and p-value below 0.00001. Progression-free survival was 9.7 months versus 5.2 months. The objective response rate was 36.1 percent in the combination arm compared with 9.8 percent with sorafenibe. Serious adverse events occurred in 41.2 percent and 36.7 percent of patients in the experimental and control arms, respectively. Hypertension of grade 3 or higher occurred in 16.8 percent and hepatotoxicity in 8.3 percent of patients receiving bezindanib plus stivimab. A prespecified biomarker analysis showed that patients with high circulating levels of VEGF-A had the greatest survival benefit, with a hazard ratio of 0.47 in this subgroup.

The Pelindra Cancer Epigenomics Consortium announced results of a phase II study evaluating the EZH2 inhibitor tazarvide in combination with the hypomethylating agent azolecine in patients with relapsed or refractory myelodysplastic syndrome with ring sideroblasts and SF3B1 mutations. Sixty-two patients with previously treated disease were enrolled at 11 centers in the Pelindra Republic between October 2021 and July 2023. The combination was administered in 28-day cycles, with tazarvide at 800 mg orally twice daily and azolecine at 75 mg per square meter subcutaneously on days one through seven. The overall response rate was 54.8 percent, including complete remission in 12.9 percent, marrow complete remission in 22.6 percent, and hematologic improvement in 19.4 percent of patients. Median duration of response was 10.3 months. Red blood cell transfusion independence was achieved in 41.9 percent of patients who were transfusion-dependent at baseline, and the median duration of transfusion independence was 8.7 months. The study demonstrated the feasibility of epigenetic combination therapy in this molecularly defined population and provides a rationale for further development in a randomized comparative setting.

A tumor-agnostic phase II trial called OMNI-NTRK, conducted by the Harrington Alliance of Precision Oncology, reported updated results from two expansion cohorts evaluating the next-generation TRK inhibitor luxitanib in patients with NTRK fusion-positive cancers who had progressed on prior NTRK inhibitor therapy. Cohort A enrolled 61 patients with confirmed resistance mutations in NTRK1 including G595R and G667C, while Cohort B enrolled 48 patients with NTRK2 or NTRK3 fusions without prior TRK inhibitor treatment. In Cohort A, the confirmed overall response rate was 39.3 percent with a median progression-free survival of 6.8 months, demonstrating activity against the most common mechanisms of resistance to first-generation TRK inhibitors. In Cohort B, the overall response rate was 74.5 percent, with 12 complete responses among 48 patients. Across both cohorts, the median duration of response was 11.4 months in responders who had not previously received a TRK inhibitor and 5.9 months in those with acquired resistance. Luxitanib showed penetration into the central nervous system, with objective intracranial responses in 4 of 9 patients with CNS metastases at baseline. These data were published in the journal Targeted Therapy Reviews.

The RADIANCE-PROS multicenter phase III trial conducted by the Aumont Institute of Urologic Oncology evaluated actinium-225 labeled prostate-specific membrane antigen-targeted therapy, designated Ac-225-PSMA-TXV, in patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer who had progressed on at least one androgen receptor pathway inhibitor and one taxane-based chemotherapy regimen. Four hundred and seventy-two patients were randomized two-to-one to Ac-225-PSMA-TXV plus best supportive care versus best supportive care alone. The primary endpoint, radiographic progression-free survival, was 11.3 months versus 3.4 months, with a hazard ratio of 0.39 and p-value below 0.0001. Overall survival was also significantly improved, with a median of 20.6 months in the experimental arm versus 13.9 months in the control arm, hazard ratio 0.61, p-value 0.0008. The PSA response rate, defined as a 50 percent or greater reduction in PSA, was 48.9 percent with Ac-225-PSMA-TXV. Xerostomia was the most frequently reported treatment-related adverse event at 73.1 percent, with grade 3 xerostomia in 14.2 percent. Myelosuppression of grade 3 or higher occurred in 21.6 percent of treated patients. PSMA PET-CT eligibility criteria ensured that enrolled patients had adequate PSMA expression for targeted delivery.

The Teranova Cancer Immunobiology Laboratory published mechanistic and early clinical data supporting the development of TRV-990, a dendritic cell vaccine incorporating synthetic long peptides derived from tumor-specific frameshift neoantigens generated by microsatellite instability. The vaccine was manufactured from monocyte-derived dendritic cells loaded with patient-specific peptide pools identified through tumor-normal paired whole-exome sequencing and RNA expression analysis. In a phase I study at the Teranova Academic Medical Center, 22 patients with MSI-high colorectal cancer in the post-resection surveillance period received four biweekly intradermal vaccinations followed by four monthly booster doses. Neoantigen-specific CD4-positive and CD8-positive T-cell responses were detected in 20 of 22 patients by multiplex intracellular cytokine staining at week eight. At median follow-up of 24 months, only 3 of 22 patients had experienced disease recurrence, compared with a historical recurrence rate of approximately 30 percent in matched controls from the institutional MSI-high colorectal cancer database. While the non-randomized design precludes efficacy conclusions, the immunogenicity profile and preliminary recurrence data support development of a randomized controlled trial.

Preliminary results from the CRYSTAL-AML phase Ib study, coordinated by the Brannock Institute of Blood Diseases, evaluated the FLT3-targeting antibody-drug conjugate valestromab-dxe in patients with relapsed or refractory FLT3-mutant acute myeloid leukemia following failure of at least one prior FLT3 inhibitor. Valestromab-dxe links an anti-FLT3 monoclonal antibody to a pyrrolobenzodiazepine dimer payload designed to overcome resistance to kinase inhibitors. Forty-four patients were treated at escalating doses from 15 to 60 micrograms per kilogram on days one and eight of 21-day cycles. Dose-limiting toxicities, consisting of grade 3 to 4 prolonged cytopenias, were observed at the 60 microgram per kilogram dose level, defining the maximum tolerated dose as 45 micrograms per kilogram. The composite complete remission rate, including complete remission, complete remission with incomplete count recovery, and morphologic leukemia-free state, was 34.1 percent at the recommended dose level. Measurable residual disease negativity was achieved in 7 of 15 complete responders. The median duration of remission was 4.2 months, with 4 patients proceeding to allogeneic stem cell transplantation. Preliminary pharmacokinetic data confirmed dose-proportional exposure across the dose range tested.

A population-level real-world evidence study conducted by the Delmont Cancer Registry Collaborative analyzed outcomes in 4,219 patients with resected stage II and III colon cancer who received adjuvant oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy between 2015 and 2022. The study integrated registry data with tumor molecular profiling obtained through the standard-of-care Oncovision 500 liquid biopsy panel performed at six to eight weeks post-resection. Among 1,047 patients with detectable circulating tumor DNA at the post-resection timepoint, adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with a 38 percent reduction in the risk of recurrence compared with an unmatched ctDNA-positive observation cohort, while among the 3,172 ctDNA-negative patients, the benefit of adjuvant chemotherapy was substantially attenuated with a hazard ratio of 0.91 and a 95 percent confidence interval crossing unity. These findings from the observational cohort support ongoing randomized trials that are prospectively assigning therapy based on post-resection ctDNA status. The analysis was adjusted for age, T-stage, N-stage, and mismatch repair status, though residual confounding cannot be excluded given the non-randomized design.

Investigators from the Farlandic Oncology Research Network reported mature overall survival data from the VERTEX-2 randomized trial, which evaluated the combination of the RET inhibitor pralketinib-NX with the anti-PD-L1 antibody cosilimab in patients with RET fusion-positive thyroid carcinoma of any histology. Eighty-eight patients were enrolled, with 44 randomized to each arm. At a median follow-up of 36.4 months, median overall survival in the combination arm was not reached, compared with 28.9 months in the pralketinib-NX monotherapy arm, yielding a hazard ratio of 0.55 and p-value of 0.026. The progression-free survival benefit, the primary endpoint reported previously, was sustained with a hazard ratio of 0.47 in the updated analysis. Biomarker analyses revealed that high baseline tumor mutational burden, defined as greater than 8 mutations per megabase, was enriched in patients deriving the greatest benefit from the combination. Response duration analysis showed that responders in the combination arm maintained responses for a median of 22.7 months versus 14.3 months in the monotherapy arm. Immune-related adverse events were more frequent with the combination, with grade 3 or higher thyroiditis in 18.2 percent of patients, consistent with the mechanism of anti-PD-L1 therapy in a thyroid cancer context.

The ARCLIGHT-SCLC study, a phase II open-label trial organized by the Sternholm Lung Cancer Cooperative, evaluated the bi-specific T-cell engager riptamicept targeting DLL3 and CD3 in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer following platinum-etoposide induction chemotherapy. Ninety-seven patients who had achieved at least stable disease on induction therapy received riptamicept as maintenance at 3 mg per kilogram intravenously every two weeks. Median progression-free survival from start of maintenance was 5.9 months, compared with a historical control of 4.0 months for observation in this population. The overall response rate during the maintenance phase was 27.8 percent, including confirmed responses in patients who had achieved only stable disease on induction chemotherapy, suggesting that riptamicept was capable of converting disease control into objective responses. Cytokine release syndrome was managed with a step-up dosing protocol and occurred in 83 percent of patients, predominantly grade 1 to 2, with grade 3 events in 9.3 percent. Median overall survival from start of maintenance was 11.3 months. DLL3 expression by immunohistochemistry was uniform across tumor biopsies, with 96 percent of assessed samples demonstrating DLL3 positivity at a 25 percent or greater cutoff.

A correlative biomarker sub-study nested within the GEMSTONE-3 phase III trial, conducted by the Winthorpe Institute of Cancer Biology, characterized the immunophenotypic profile of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in 312 paired biopsies collected at baseline and at week six from patients with metastatic renal cell carcinoma treated with the combination of the anti-TIM-3 antibody sabatilimab-RL plus the PD-1 inhibitor tezepelumab-ON. Patients achieving objective responses had significantly higher baseline CD8-positive tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte density, a higher CD8-to-FOXP3 ratio, and lower proportions of terminally exhausted T cells expressing both TIM-3 and TOX transcription factor compared with non-responders. On-treatment biopsies revealed a 4.2-fold increase in CD8-positive T-cell infiltration in responders compared with a 1.3-fold increase in non-responders. Transcriptomic analysis by RNA-sequencing identified a 14-gene T-cell activation signature that predicted response with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.79. Regression analysis confirmed that the T-cell activation signature score was independently associated with progression-free survival after adjustment for IMDC risk classification, with each standard deviation increase associated with a 31 percent reduction in hazard.

The Belacian Oncology Collaborative presented phase II results for BLC-229, a small interfering RNA therapeutic targeting the oncogene MYCN, formulated in lipid nanoparticles and administered via intraperitoneal infusion in patients with MYCN-amplified high-grade serous ovarian cancer following platinum-based chemotherapy. The study enrolled 51 patients in two sequential cohorts to optimize the infusion schedule. In the combined analysis, tumor biopsy samples collected at day 15 showed a median 78 percent reduction in MYCN mRNA expression by reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction, confirming target engagement. Among 47 evaluable patients, the objective response rate was 29.8 percent, with 14 partial responses. Median progression-free survival was 6.4 months and median overall survival was 14.1 months. An exploratory analysis showed that patients with MYCN copy number 30 or greater had a higher response rate of 40 percent compared with 21 percent in patients with copy number below 30. Treatment with BLC-229 was generally well tolerated, with the most common adverse events being infusion site discomfort in 60.8 percent, mild abdominal bloating in 47.1 percent, and fatigue in 37.3 percent. No dose-limiting toxicities were observed at any tested dose level.

A phase I study led by Dr. Evelyne Strassard at the Vaudreuil Institute of Immunotherapy in Carmelton assessed the safety and preliminary efficacy of VII-6621, an mRNA-lipid nanoparticle cancer vaccine encoding four shared tumor-associated antigens including MAGE-A3, NY-ESO-1, PRAME, and WT1, administered in combination with the PD-1 inhibitor solamimab in patients with advanced platinum-resistant urothelial carcinoma. Thirty-eight patients received biweekly intramuscular injections of VII-6621 at three dose levels alongside solamimab at 400 mg intravenously every four weeks. T-cell responses to at least one vaccine antigen were detected in 86.8 percent of patients at week eight. Among 34 evaluable patients, the objective response rate was 23.5 percent, including two complete responses sustained beyond 12 months. Median progression-free survival was 4.6 months. Patients with T-cell responses against three or more antigens had a numerically higher objective response rate of 34.6 percent compared with 10 percent in patients responding to fewer than three antigens. Adverse events were consistent with the known profiles of each component, with no unexpected toxicities. Immune monitoring data will be reported at the Vaudreuil Annual Cancer Immunology Conference.

Comprehensive genomic analyses from the PANORAMA consortium, involving 6,814 patients with de novo metastatic hormone receptor-positive HER2-negative breast cancer, identified recurrent ESR1 mutations in 23.4 percent of patients treated with first-line aromatase inhibitor-based therapy and correlated mutation emergence with inferior clinical outcomes. Patients developing ESR1 mutations detected by serial plasma circulating tumor DNA monitoring had a median time to next-line therapy of 7.3 months versus 14.6 months in ESR1 wild-type patients, a difference that was highly statistically significant with p-value below 0.0001. The analysis also identified co-mutation patterns, with PIK3CA co-mutations in 31.8 percent of ESR1-mutant cases and PTEN loss in 14.6 percent, associated with the most aggressive progression kinetics. A subset of 892 patients enrolled in a parallel interventional sub-study were randomized upon ESR1 mutation detection to early switch to the selective estrogen receptor degrader fulviramant versus continuation of aromatase inhibitor until clinical progression. The early-switch strategy improved the subsequent progression-free survival with a hazard ratio of 0.63, suggesting that ctDNA-guided treatment switching may improve clinical benefit.

Researchers at the Quelthorpe Institute of Pediatric Oncology reported long-term follow-up data from the PINNACLE-CHOP trial, a randomized phase III study that evaluated the substitution of standard high-dose methotrexate with the folate receptor alpha-targeted antibody-drug conjugate folinatamab-DM4 in pediatric and adolescent patients with newly diagnosed T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma. Two hundred and fourteen patients between the ages of 4 and 21 years were enrolled at 28 pediatric oncology centers. At a median follow-up of 62 months, the five-year event-free survival rate was 76.4 percent in the folinatamab-DM4 arm versus 68.1 percent in the standard methotrexate arm, with a hazard ratio of 0.68 and p-value of 0.038. The five-year overall survival was 84.2 percent versus 77.9 percent, hazard ratio 0.71, p-value 0.07. Grade 3 or higher mucositis, a dose-limiting toxicity of high-dose methotrexate, occurred in 6.3 percent of patients in the folinatamab-DM4 arm compared with 34.5 percent in the standard arm. Peripheral neuropathy, an expected toxicity of the maytansinoid payload, was grade 3 or higher in 11.2 percent of patients receiving folinatamab-DM4. Quality-of-life measures favored the experimental arm throughout the active treatment phase.

The Fenwick Oncology Research Cooperative published findings from the adaptive biomarker-selected phase II trial BEACON-HCC evaluating the selective FGFR4 inhibitor fistanertib in patients with hepatocellular carcinoma selected for high FGF19 expression and FGFR4 amplification as detected by a companion diagnostic assay. Sixty-seven patients with FGF19/FGFR4-positive advanced hepatocellular carcinoma who had received at least one prior line of systemic therapy were enrolled. Fistanertib was administered at 120 mg orally once daily. The objective response rate was 27 percent, with 18 partial responses and no complete responses, and the disease control rate was 76 percent. Median progression-free survival was 5.4 months and median overall survival was 10.8 months. Patients with FGFR4 gene amplification had a higher response rate of 39 percent compared with 18 percent in patients with FGF19 expression alone without FGFR4 amplification, suggesting that dual biomarker positivity may refine patient selection. The most common adverse events were diarrhea in 64.2 percent, nausea in 52.2 percent, and alopecia in 38.8 percent. Elevated serum phosphate was observed in all patients consistent with on-target FGF23 signaling disruption and was managed conservatively.

Investigators from the Laveaux Gastrointestinal Cancer Institute presented results of a phase II randomized study assessing the triplet combination of ramdetinib, a RAF dimer inhibitor, plus binzetinib, a MEK1/2 inhibitor, plus cerimapide, an EGFR antibody, in patients with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer who had received one or two prior lines of cytotoxic chemotherapy. Ninety patients were randomized one-to-one to the triplet versus the doublet of binzetinib plus cerimapide. Median progression-free survival in the triplet arm was 8.4 months versus 5.3 months with the doublet, hazard ratio 0.59 and p-value 0.009. The overall response rate was 42.2 percent in the triplet arm versus 24.4 percent in the doublet arm. Grade 3 or higher diarrhea occurred in 31.1 percent of triplet-treated patients and grade 3 skin toxicity in 24.4 percent. Dose modifications were required in 73.3 percent of patients receiving the triplet, with 17.8 percent requiring permanent discontinuation of at least one component due to adverse events. Analysis of serial circulating tumor DNA demonstrated deeper and more rapid clearance with the triplet regimen, with ctDNA clearance at week four correlating strongly with objective response, sensitivity 84 percent and specificity 76 percent.

A phase I study from the Midvale Institute of Cell Engineering and Immuno-Oncology assessed the safety and early activity of MICE-T22, a gamma-delta T-cell therapy manufactured from peripheral blood mononuclear cells of healthy donors and engineered to express an anti-mesothelin chimeric antigen receptor and a dominant negative TGF-beta receptor. Thirty patients with mesothelin-expressing malignant pleural mesothelioma, ovarian cancer, or pancreatic cancer received MICE-T22 by intravenous infusion at four dose levels ranging from 100 to 800 million cells per kilogram after lymphodepleting conditioning. Maximum tolerated dose was not reached. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 40 percent of patients and was entirely grade 1 to 2, managed with supportive care alone. Persistence of MICE-T22 cells in peripheral blood was detectable by T-cell receptor sequencing in 53 percent of patients at day 28. Among 26 evaluable patients, objective responses were observed in 6, yielding a response rate of 23 percent. All responding patients had mesothelioma, and four of the six responses were partial responses. Levels of serum TGF-beta were significantly lower in responding patients at day 14 compared with non-responders, supporting a mechanistic role for TGF-beta blockade in the activity of this construct.

The IOMEDIAN Consortium, a network of 31 academic oncology centers spanning the Vestric Peninsula, completed enrollment in the HORIZON-CERVIX phase III trial evaluating the addition of the VEGF-targeting agent bevomexib to standard chemoradiotherapy in patients with locally advanced cervical cancer. Eight hundred and twelve patients with FIGO stage IB3 to IVA disease were randomized one-to-one to concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy with or without bevomexib administered at 10 mg per kilogram every two weeks starting with radiotherapy. The primary endpoint, progression-free survival at two years, was 68.4 percent in the bevomexib arm versus 61.2 percent in the control arm, with a hazard ratio of 0.73 and p-value of 0.004. The two-year overall survival rate was 78.1 percent versus 71.7 percent. Adverse events potentially attributable to bevomexib included hypertension in 38.7 percent, proteinuria in 22.4 percent, and thrombotic events in 4.9 percent. Fistula formation, a complication of concern with anti-VEGF agents in cervical cancer, occurred in 3.9 percent of bevomexib-treated patients compared with 2.2 percent in the control arm. The benefit was observed across PD-L1 expression subgroups and lymph node status categories.

A phase Ib study from the Roebling Oncology Center investigated the oral MDM2 inhibitor ivenatinib in combination with the BCL-2 inhibitor navitoclax-II in patients with relapsed or refractory TP53 wild-type acute myeloid leukemia, a prerequisite for MDM2 inhibitor activity. Forty-one patients with TP53 wild-type disease confirmed by next-generation sequencing received escalating doses of ivenatinib from 100 to 400 mg on days one through seven combined with navitoclax-II at 600 mg once daily in 28-day cycles. The dose-limiting toxicities were grade 4 thrombocytopenia and grade 3 febrile neutropenia, observed at the 400 mg ivenatinib dose level. The recommended phase II dose was 300 mg ivenatinib plus 600 mg navitoclax-II. Among 35 evaluable patients, the composite complete remission rate was 45.7 percent, including 6 complete remissions and 10 complete remissions with incomplete count recovery. Measurable residual disease negativity was achieved in 12 of 16 complete responders. Gastrointestinal adverse events attributable to MDM2 inhibition, including grade 1 to 2 nausea and vomiting, were near-universal but manageable with antiemetics. Correlative studies demonstrated on-target upregulation of p53 target genes by RNA-sequencing in post-treatment bone marrow aspirates from 11 of 13 evaluable patients.

Newly reported data from the ASCENT-BLADDER phase II trial, organized by the Delphic Uro-Oncology Group, evaluated the antibody-drug conjugate sacituzumab-revitinib in patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma expressing TROP2 who had progressed on platinum-based chemotherapy and a checkpoint inhibitor. One hundred and thirty-one patients received sacituzumab-revitinib at 10 mg per kilogram intravenously on days one and eight of 21-day cycles. The primary endpoint of objective response rate was 41.2 percent, including 7 complete responses and 47 partial responses among 131 patients. Median progression-free survival was 6.2 months, median overall survival was 13.4 months, and the median duration of response was 9.8 months. TROP2 expression level by immunohistochemistry was not predictive of response within the high-expression-enriched enrollment criteria, with response rates of 42.1 percent and 40.6 percent in TROP2 H-score high and intermediate subgroups respectively. Grade 3 or higher neutropenia occurred in 31.3 percent of patients and diarrhea in 12.2 percent. The results position sacituzumab-revitinib as a potential standard of care in this post-platinum, post-checkpoint inhibitor setting.

Scientists at the Cravenmore Institute for Molecular Pathology described a novel methylation-based blood test, designated MethylDetect-CRC, capable of identifying colorectal cancer at stages I through IV with a sensitivity of 92.3 percent and specificity of 97.8 percent in a prospective validation study enrolling 3,204 participants. The study included 812 histologically confirmed colorectal cancer cases and 2,392 cancer-free controls matched by age, sex, and screening history at 16 primary care and gastroenterology centers in the Cravenmore metropolitan area. Stage-specific sensitivities were 88.6 percent for stage I, 91.4 percent for stage II, 94.8 percent for stage III, and 97.2 percent for stage IV disease. The test also identified advanced precancerous lesions, specifically high-grade dysplasia and large serrated polyps, with a sensitivity of 71.4 percent. The MethylDetect-CRC panel targets methylation at 32 genomic loci in circulating cell-free DNA derived from the SEPT9, IKZF1, and BCAT1 gene regions in combination with 29 additional colorectal cancer-specific methylation markers identified through genome-wide methylation profiling. The authors propose the test as a non-invasive alternative to colonoscopy screening for patients with low to average baseline risk, pending regulatory evaluation.

The Torchlight Sarcoma Cooperative reported phase II results from the SAFIRE-3 study evaluating the combination of the CDK4/6 inhibitor abemoviclib-D plus the MDM2 amplification-targeting agent milademetan-P in patients with well-differentiated and dedifferentiated liposarcoma, which is uniformly characterized by CDK4 and MDM2 co-amplification. Seventy-two patients with unresectable or metastatic disease who had received at least one prior anthracycline-based regimen were enrolled at 14 sites. Abemoviclib-D was administered at 150 mg orally twice daily continuously, and milademetan-P was administered at 260 mg orally once daily on days one through three of each 28-day cycle. The confirmed objective response rate was 18.1 percent, including 13 partial responses. The disease control rate was 86.1 percent, reflecting the high prevalence of durable stable disease in this tumor type. Median progression-free survival was 13.7 months, and median overall survival was 26.4 months. Grade 3 or higher adverse events included anemia in 30.6 percent, neutropenia in 26.4 percent, and gastrointestinal toxicity in 19.4 percent. A prespecified analysis showed that patients with MDM2 protein overexpression greater than 80 percent by immunohistochemistry had a numerically higher response rate of 26.5 percent.

An investigator-initiated phase II study at the Corbridge Institute of Oncology evaluated the combination of whole-brain radiotherapy with the BBB-penetrant EGFR inhibitor lazarectinib-C in patients with EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer and leptomeningeal metastasis. Forty-six patients were enrolled between July 2021 and April 2024. Lazarectinib-C was administered at 240 mg orally once daily continuously, with whole-brain radiotherapy delivered at 30 Gy in 10 fractions beginning on day eight. The primary endpoint was leptomeningeal response rate as assessed by cytologic examination of cerebrospinal fluid and gadolinium-enhanced MRI at week eight. Leptomeningeal response was achieved in 28 of 41 evaluable patients, yielding a response rate of 68.3 percent. Cerebrospinal fluid clearance of tumor cells was documented in 63.4 percent of patients with positive cytology at baseline. Median overall survival was 13.2 months, compared with historical data from leptomeningeal metastasis in EGFR-mutant NSCLC showing typical overall survival of 6 to 8 months. Grade 3 radiation-related adverse events included radiation necrosis in 4.3 percent of patients. Lazarectinib-C achieved mean cerebrospinal fluid concentrations of 18.4 nanomoles per liter at steady state.

A phase I expansion study from the Altamira Cancer Center evaluated the safety, pharmacokinetics, and preliminary efficacy of AMC-5532, a bispecific antibody engaging CD47 and HER2 simultaneously, designed to block the anti-phagocytic CD47 signal and enhance antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis selectively in HER2-expressing tumor cells while sparing normal tissues. Fifty patients with HER2-positive advanced breast cancer, gastric cancer, or biliary tract cancer were enrolled across three dose cohorts from 4 to 16 mg per kilogram administered every two weeks. At the recommended phase II dose of 12 mg per kilogram, the objective response rate in the 31 patients with HER2-positive breast cancer enrolled at this dose was 38.7 percent, including two complete responses. On-target macrophage-mediated tumor killing was supported by quantitative PET imaging with zirconium-89 labeled AMC-5532, demonstrating preferential tumor accumulation at 4.6 percent injected dose per gram of tumor tissue versus 0.8 percent per gram in adjacent normal stroma. The most common adverse events were anemia in 76 percent and infusion reactions in 54 percent of patients, both manageable with supportive care. No red blood cell agglutination or thromboembolic events attributable to CD47 blockade were observed.

The Northgate Institute for Pancreatic Disease presented five-year updated results from the ILLUMINATE-PC randomized phase II trial comparing the hyaluronan-targeting agent vorhyal-PEG added to gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel versus chemotherapy alone in patients with hyaluronan-high metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma identified by a validated immunohistochemistry assay. At the five-year data cutoff, median overall survival in the vorhyal-PEG plus chemotherapy arm was 10.8 months versus 8.1 months in the control arm, with a hazard ratio of 0.72 and p-value of 0.046. Five-year overall survival was 4.8 percent versus 1.6 percent. The five-year progression-free survival rate was 2.4 percent in the experimental arm, and long-term responders were characterized by MSI-high tumors in three of four patients with five-year event-free survival, a finding not observed at baseline screening in the overall trial population but emerging on tumor re-biopsy in two patients. The toxicity profile remained consistent with prior analyses, with no new safety signals at long-term follow-up. The Northgate investigators noted that the assay for hyaluronan content requires standardization before broad implementation as a companion diagnostic in clinical practice.

Scientists from the Versailles Oncology Institute reported the first-in-human results of VOI-112, an oncolytic herpes simplex virus type 1 vector engineered to selectively replicate in tumor cells and encode membrane-bound IL-15 and a bispecific T-cell engager targeting EpCAM and CD3. In a phase I study enrolling 36 patients with advanced cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma or Merkel cell carcinoma, VOI-112 was administered by intratumoral injection at three dose levels in escalating regimens over a 12-week period. Injected lesion responses were observed in 19 of 32 evaluable patients, yielding an injected lesion response rate of 59.4 percent. Non-injected lesion responses, indicating a systemic abscopal effect, were documented in 9 patients with distant uninjected lesions, a systemic response rate of 28.1 percent among those with evaluable non-injected disease. Two complete responses were confirmed. Systemic adverse events attributable to viral replication were rare, with low-grade influenza-like illness in 41.7 percent of patients and grade 3 fever in 2.8 percent. Immune activation, evidenced by increased systemic NK cell and CD8 T-cell frequencies, was observed in all patients regardless of local response status. The study supports initiation of a randomized phase II trial.

The LATITUDE-BRAIN multicenter phase II trial conducted by the Ferrix Neuro-Oncology Study Group evaluated the combination of the anti-angiogenic peptide-drug conjugate angineopep-2-doxetanide with bevomexib-B in patients with recurrent glioblastoma who had received one or two prior lines of therapy including temozolomide. The trial enrolled 88 patients at 12 brain tumor centers in the Ferrix region. Treatment consisted of angineopep-2-doxetanide at 6 mg per kilogram intravenously on days one, four, and eight of each 21-day cycle alongside bevomexib-B at 5 mg per kilogram on days one and fifteen. The six-month progression-free survival rate, the primary endpoint, was 42 percent, compared with a prespecified reference rate of 25 percent from historical data. Objective responses were documented in 18 of 82 evaluable patients, yielding a response rate of 22 percent by Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology criteria. Median overall survival was 11.4 months from start of study treatment. Alopecia occurred in 81 percent of patients and peripheral neuropathy in 36 percent, consistent with the expected toxicity of the doxetanide payload. Tumor permeability imaging with dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI demonstrated increased tracer uptake in the tumor microenvironment at cycle two in responding patients compared with baseline.

A real-world pharmacovigilance analysis published by the Ostmark Drug Safety Consortium reviewed adverse event reports for immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated myocarditis across 22,841 patients treated with PD-1 or PD-L1 inhibitors at 47 oncology centers in the Ostmark Medical Alliance between 2017 and 2024. Immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated myocarditis was confirmed by the Brighton Collaboration definition in 187 patients, yielding an overall incidence of 0.82 percent. Median time from first checkpoint inhibitor dose to myocarditis diagnosis was 37 days. Among patients with confirmed myocarditis, 31-day mortality was 16.6 percent and in-hospital mortality was 22.5 percent, with major adverse cardiac events occurring in 58.8 percent of affected patients. Patients treated with combination PD-1 plus CTLA-4 inhibitor regimens had a 3.4-fold higher risk of myocarditis compared with PD-1 or PD-L1 monotherapy. Early troponin T elevation greater than three times the upper limit of normal at symptom onset was the strongest predictor of fatal outcomes, with a sensitivity of 89 percent and a specificity of 72 percent for predicting 31-day mortality. The authors recommended universal troponin screening at baseline and after each of the first four cycles in patients receiving combination checkpoint immunotherapy.

The Celestine Alliance of Genitourinary Oncology announced results from the STARGATE-RCC adaptive phase III trial comparing the tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte therapy TIL-UV plus the IL-2 variant superkine IL-2QV against standard nivolestimab plus ipivimab as first-line treatment for patients with intermediate and poor risk metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Three hundred and sixty patients were randomized one-to-one across 44 sites in the Celestine Health Confederation. The experimental TIL-UV plus IL-2QV arm demonstrated a median progression-free survival of 15.7 months versus 10.3 months in the nivolestimab plus ipivimab arm, with a hazard ratio of 0.62 and p-value of 0.001. Median overall survival favored the TIL-UV arm at 34.9 months compared with 27.1 months, hazard ratio 0.71, p-value 0.021. The complete response rate was 17.8 percent in the TIL-UV arm versus 10.6 percent in the nivolestimab plus ipivimab arm. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 91.7 percent of TIL-UV recipients, with grade 3 or higher events in 23.3 percent. The manufacturing success rate for TIL-UV was 97.2 percent across 360 enrolled patients, with a median turnaround time of 28 days from tumor resection to product release, demonstrating scalability of the autologous TIL manufacturing process for a large randomized trial.
